content.description:
Quick-reference recipes for writing common items in [Hob](item://c0c0c0c0-0054-0000-0000-000000000000). Each recipe shows the Hob code you'd put in `content.hob` (for views) or `content.code` (for code items). ## Three ways to write a view There are three ways to define a view, depending on which item type and content field you use: | Approach | Item type | Content field | You write | Kernel does | |----------|-----------|---------------|-----------|-------------| | **Hob view** | `view` | `content.hob` | Just the render expression | Wraps it in a function, injects `item` and `api` into scope | | **JS view** | `view` | `content.code` | Full ES module with `export async function render(item, api)` | Loads as module, calls `.render()` | | **def-view** | `code` | `content.code` | Hob code using `def-view` macro | Reads `:for-type` and `:render` from the exported map | A Hob view is the most concise ‚Äî the kernel already knows it's a view (from the item type) so the code is just the body of the render function. A JS view is a full module that must export its own `render`. The `def-view` macro is for when you want a Hob view inside a code item (where the item type is `code`, not `view`, so you need to explicitly declare the type binding). ## View (Hob) Put the Hob expression in `content.hob` on a view item (type `view`). The kernel wraps this into a render function and injects `item` and `api` ‚Äî just write the expression that returns hiccup. ```clojure ;; Simple note view ‚Äî shows title + body (let [note (get-item (:id item))] [:div {:style "padding: 16px;"} [:h1 (:name note)] [:div {:style "white-space: pre-wrap;"} (or (get-in note [:content :description]) "(empty)")]]) ``` Call `get-item` during render to register the item as a reactivity dependency ‚Äî the view auto-refreshes when it changes. ### View with tags ```clojure (let [note (get-item (:id item)) tags (or (get-in note [:content :tags]) [])] [:div {:style "padding: 16px;"} [:h2 (:name note)] (when (seq tags) [:div {:style "display: flex; gap: 4px; margin-bottom: 8px;"} (for [tag-id tags] (let [tag (get-item tag-id)] [:span {:style "background: #e0e0e0; padding: 2px 8px; border-radius: 4px; font-size: 0.8em;"} (:name tag)]))]) [:div (or (get-in note [:content :description]) "(empty)")]]) ``` ### View with click handlers ```clojure (let [note (get-item (:id item)) counter (atom 0)] [:div {:style "padding: 16px;"} [:h2 (:name note)] [:button {:on-click (fn [e] (swap! counter inc))} (str "Clicked " (deref counter) " times")]]) ``` ### View with conditional rendering ```clojure (let [note (get-item (:id item)) body (get-in note [:content :description])] [:div {:style "padding: 16px;"} [:h2 (:name note)] (if body [:div {:style "white-space: pre-wrap;"} body] [:p {:style "color: #999; font-style: italic;"} "No content yet."])]) ``` ### View with child items ```clojure (let [note (get-item (:id item)) children (:attachments note)] [:div {:style "padding: 16px;"} [:h2 (:name note)] (when (seq children) [:div {:style "margin-top: 12px;"} (for [child-id children] (render-item child-id))])]) ``` `render-item` returns a DOM element for the child, rendered with its own best-matching view. ## View (def-view macro) Use `def-view` when writing a view inside a code item (type `code`). Since the item type is `code` rather than `view`, the kernel won't automatically treat it as a view ‚Äî the macro explicitly declares the type binding and render function: ```clojure (def-view my-note-view @note-type [item api] [:div.note [:h2 (:name item)] [:p (.. item :content :description)]]) ``` This expands to `(def my-note-view {:for-type @note-type :render (fn [item api] ...)})`. The kernel reads `:for-type` and `:render` from the resulting map to register the view. ## Watch A watch reacts to events. Use the `def-watch` macro: ```clojure ;; Log when any note is updated (def-watch note-logger {:event :item/updated :type @note-type} [event api] (log (str "Note changed: " (.. (:item event) :name)))) ``` ### Watch with type-extends filter ```clojure ;; React to changes on items whose type chain includes @note-type (def-watch note-chain-watcher {:event :item/updated :type-extends @note-type} [event api] (let [item (:item event) prev (:previous event)] (when (not= (.. item :content :title) (.. prev :content :title)) (log "Title changed!")))) ``` ### Watch that emits another event ```clojure (def-watch tag-counter {:event :item/updated :type @note-type} [event api] (let [tags (or (.. (:item event) :content :tags) [])] (emit! {:type "tag-count-changed" :content {:count (count tags)}}))) ``` ## Library A library exports reusable functions. Other items load it with `(require lib-name)`. ```clojure ;; Library: string-helpers (defn truncate [s max-len] (if (<= (count s) max-len) s (str (subs s 0 (- max-len 3)) "..."))) (defn slug [s] (-> s str/lower-case (str/replace " " "-"))) (export truncate slug) ``` Consumers: ```clojure (require string-helpers) (string-helpers/truncate "A very long title" 20) ;; or destructured: (require [truncate slug] from string-helpers) (truncate "Hello world" 8) ;; => "Hello..." ``` ## Event handler function A plain function that handles an event ‚Äî typically referenced from a watch or called directly: ```clojure (defn on-kernel-boot-complete [event api] (log "System booted!")) ``` Handler names follow the convention `on-{event-name}` with kebab-case. ## Hiccup quick reference | Pattern | Result | |---------|--------| | `[:div "text"]` | `
text
` | | `[:div.my-class "text"]` | `
text
` | | `[:div#main "text"]` | `
text
` | | `[:div {:style "color: red;"} "text"]` | `
text
` | | `[:input {:type "text" :value v :on-input handler}]` | `` with handler | | `(for [x items] [:li x])` | list of `
  • ` elements | | `(when cond [:div "shown"])` | conditional element (nil = omitted) | | `(render-item id)` | embedded child view | ## View operations These functions are available inside view render contexts: | Function | Effect | |----------|--------| | `(open-item! id)` | Open item contextually ‚Äî as a sibling window in spatial canvas, or navigate to root otherwise | | `(navigate! id)` | Navigate to item as root (always replaces the current root) | | `(render-item id)` | Render a child item inline, returns DOM | | `(rerender!)` | Force the current view to re-render | Use `open-item!` for click-to-open interactions (it does the right thing based on context). Use `navigate!` only when you specifically want root navigation. ```clojure ;; Clickable card that opens as sibling window [:div {:on-click (fn [e] (open-item! (:id it)))} (:name it)] ``` ## `@` vs `get-item` ‚Äî when to use which Both fetch an item and register a reactivity dependency, but they serve different purposes: - **`@name` / `@uuid`** ‚Äî reference a *known* item by name or ID. Use this for types, tags, and other well-known items whose identity you know at write time: `@note-type`, `@my-tag`. - **`(get-item id)`** ‚Äî fetch an item by a *runtime* variable. Use this inside views to re-fetch the current item being rendered, since the view parameter `item` is a plain object that doesn't register reactivity. Calling `(get-item (:id item))` tells the dependency tracker "this view depends on this item" so it auto-refreshes on changes. Rule of thumb: if you can name it, use `@`. If the ID comes from a variable (the view's `item`, a loop, a query result), use `get-item`. ## Item reference cheat sheet | Syntax | Meaning | |--------|---------| | `@my-item` | Reference a known item by name (tracked) | | `@a1b2c3d4-...` | Reference a known item by UUID (tracked) | | `(get-item id)` | Fetch item by runtime variable (tracked) | | `(. item :field)` | Access field on item or map | | `(.. item :content :title)` | Chain through references | | `(get-in item [:content :tags])` | Nested field access | | `(set-item! updated-item)` | Save item to store | | `(find-items {:type @note-type})` | Query items by type | ## State management ```clojure ;; Create mutable state (local to a view/handler) (def counter (atom 0)) ;; Read (deref counter) ;; => 0 ;; Update (swap! counter inc) ;; => 1 (swap! counter + 5) ;; => 6 (reset! counter 0) ;; => 0 ``` Atoms are for local ephemeral state. For persistent state, use `set-item!` to write back to the item store.
